content.text: The CGL portfolio delivered strong top-line growth but weaker underwriting quality. GWP increased from 2,647 to 3,015 (+368, +13.9%), NWP from 630 to 731 (+101, +16.0%), and NEP from 566 to 685 (+119, +21.0%).
Despite that growth, UW Result increased only marginally from 376 to 385 (+9, +2.4%), which means earnings did not scale with premium and NEP expansion.
The portfolio’s underwriting margin on NEP fell from 66.4% to 56.2% (-10.2 pts), indicating margin compression despite higher volume.
Claims have worsened materially. NIC Before IBNR rose from 53 to 184 (+131, +247.2%), IBNR reduced from 65 to 53 (-12, -18.5%), and NIC After IBNR doubled from 118 to 237 (+119, +100.8%).
The claims deterioration is clearly visible in ratios: Financial Loss Ratio increased from 21% to 35% (+14 pts) and Incurred LR increased from 9% to 27% (+18 pts).
Cost discipline also weakened. Expense in Mns increased from 119 to 172 (+53, +44.5%), pushing Expense Ratio from 19% to 24% (+5 pts). This is a major driver of reduced underwriting quality.

Reinsurance dependence remains high, though slightly improved. Ceding ratio moved from 76.2% to 75.8% (-0.4 pts) and retention improved from 23.8% to 24.2% (+0.4 pts). However, the book is still heavily ceded.

Reinsurance economics improved in absolute terms. RI Commission increased from 267 to 350 (+83, +31.1%), and the Commission Ratio moved from -7% to -15% (-8 pts), which is favourable for net profitability.

The strongest growth engine is Mumbai, contributing 2,096 GWP (69.5% of visible location FY26 GWP in the uploaded branch data), with GWP growth of +319 (+18.0%) and NEP growth of +41 (+16.3%). However, underwriting quality weakened: NIC After IBNR rose from 2 to 104 (+102), ILR increased from 1% to 31% (+30 pts), Expense Ratio rose from 23% to 32% (+9 pts), and UW Result fell from 328 to 264 (-64, -19.5%).
The biggest underwriting stress locations are Pune and Hyderabad.
Pune:NIC After IBNR 19 to 112 (+93, +489.5%), ILR 26% to 123% (+97 pts), UW -2 to -74 (-72), COR 94% to 238% (+144 pts)
Hyderabad:NEP 21 to 13 (-8, -38.1%), NIC After IBNR 3 to 7 (+4, +133.3%), ILR 6% to 42% (+36 pts), UW -1 to -9 (-8), Expense Ratio 37% to 88% (+51 pts), COR 113% to 177% (+64 pts)
The best underwriting-quality locations are Bangalore, Kolkata, Chennai, and Delhi:
Bangalore:COR 35% to 4% (-31 pts), UW 23 to 35 (+12, +52.2%), ILR flat at 1%
Kolkata:COR 21% to 16% (-5 pts), UW 17 to 23 (+6, +35.3%), and negative reported claims in FY26
Delhi:UW 0 to 112, COR 94% to 39% (-55 pts), driven by sharply lower claims and much lower ceding

Segment-wise, corp remains the core book and largest driver of results: GWP 2,355 to 2,667 (+312, +13.3%), NEP 368 to 430 (+62, +16.8%), UW 323 to 321 (-2). However, ILR worsened from 6% to 26% (+20 pts) and Expense Ratio rose from 23% to 29% (+6 pts), indicating pressure beneath the surface.

Retail is the cleanest positive turnaround among visible verticals: GWP 211 to 270 (+59, +28.0%), NEP 145 to 194 (+49, +33.8%), NIC After IBNR 90 to 28 (-62, -68.9%), ILR 25% to 15% (-10 pts), UW -7 to 79 (+86), and COR 99% to 55% (-44 pts).
Emerging is small but healthy: flat GWP at 25, NEP 17 to 19 (+2, +11.8%), UW 6 to 10 (+4, +66.7%), COR 57% to 47% (-10 pts).
SME is the sharpest vertical deterioration: GWP 56 to 54 (-2, -3.6%), NIC After IBNR -34 to 10 (+44), ILR -22% to 5% (+27 pts), UW 54 to 14 (-40, -74.1%), and COR -61% to 66% (+127 pts).
Immediate management focus should be on:
protecting Bangalore / Chennai / Kolkata / Delhi underwriting discipline,
ensuring Mumbai growth does not continue to dilute quality, and
scaling Retail and selected corp cautiously where profitability is improving rather than simply premium increasing.
NWP increased from 630 to 731 (+101, +16.0%), slightly faster than GWP.
This means the portfolio grew both gross and net, with better retention contributing to net premium growth.
Retention ratio (NWP/GWP) improved from 23.8% to 24.2% (+0.4 pts).
Ceding ratio ((Prop + Non-prop)/GWP) reduced from 76.2% to 75.8% (-0.4 pts).
Even though absolute ceding rose with premium growth, net retention improved slightly because net premium grew faster.

Business implication: reinsurance dependence is still structurally high. This portfolio is not weakly reinsured, but the book remains heavily ceded and therefore highly sensitive to reinsurance economics and commission support.
NEP increased from 566 to 685 (+119, +21.0%), which is faster than both GWP and NWP growth.

Possible interpretation based on available data: the portfolio is earning through faster on a larger book, which helped NEP growth, but that benefit did not translate proportionately into underwriting profit because claims and expenses rose sharply.
This is the most important worsening in the portfolio.
IBNR reduced, so the deterioration is not being driven by reserve strengthening.
Since IBNR reduced while NIC before IBNR rose sharply, the claims deterioration is predominantly actual claims emergence, not reserve loading.
Unlike the earlier liability examples, this book is not reserve-release driven.
underwriting deterioration is not an accounting phenomenon
This is a high-conviction underwriting signal.
Financial Loss Ratio increased from 21% to 35% (+14 pts)
Incurred LR increased from 9% to 27% (+18 pts)
This shows a genuine claims burden increase both before and after reserve consideration.
Expense Ratio increased from 19% to 24% (+5 pts)
Expense in Mns increased from 119 to 172 (+53, +44.5%)
Since NEP grew 21.0% but expense grew 44.5%, the expense burden increased much faster than earned premium.
This is comparatively stable and is not the main problem in the total portfolio.
Commission Ratio moved from -7% to -15% (-8 pts)
This is actually supportive for net profitability.
So while claims and expenses worsened, reinsurance commission economics helped cushion the portfolio outcome.
UW Result increased only slightly from 376 to 385 (+9, +2.4%)
In other words, the portfolio added volume and earned premium but produced almost no incremental underwriting profit.
The portfolio is still profitable in absolute underwriting terms, but quality has weakened materially.

children.title: Pricing / deductible / wording / referral / reinsurance implications

content.text: Pricing: immediate rate review needed in Pune, Hyderabad, and SME. Claims have risen disproportionately versus NEP.
Deductibles: consider higher deductibles / retention levels in worsening branches to protect against increased attritional and mid-sized loss burden.
Wording: review liability extensions in Pune / Hyderabad / weak SME risks where claims emergence has spiked.
Referral: make all new / renewal risks from Pune and Hyderabad referral-driven until loss quality stabilises.

Reinsurance: reinsurance support improved overall, but because the book remains heavily ceded, management should assess whether the current ceding pattern is protecting the right segments. Delhi’s ceding reduction from 56.7% to 25.9% materially altered net economics and may partly explain improved profit.

content.text: The Public Liability portfolio deteriorated materially year-on-year. GWP reduced from 274 Mn to 265 Mn (-9 Mn, -3.3%), but the underwriting result worsened from -149 Mn to -191 Mn (-42 Mn, 28.2% deeper loss) and COR deteriorated from 163% to 185% (+22 pp).

The core issue is not earned premium shortage; it is reserve-heavy claims and cost inflation. NEP was broadly flat at 231 Mn vs 230 Mn (-1 Mn, -0.4%), but IBNR increased from 182 Mn to 228 Mn (+46 Mn, +25.3%), driving NIC after IBNR up from 328 Mn to 341 Mn (+13 Mn, +4.0%) despite NIC before IBNR falling from 147 Mn to 114 Mn (-33 Mn, -22.4%).

This means underlying claims emergence looks better pre-IBNR, but the portfolio is more reserve-dependent and less certain. Incurred LR (pre-IBNR) improved from 64% to 49% (-15 pp), yet Financial Loss Ratio worsened from 142% to 148% (+6 pp) because reserve load increased.

Expenses are a second major drag. Expense ratio rose from 14% to 25% (+11 pp), with absolute expense increasing from 35 Mn to 54 Mn (+19 Mn, +54.3%). Acquisition cost also increased from 20 Mn to 35 Mn (+15 Mn, +75.0%), and acquisition cost ratio moved from 7% to 13% (+6 pp).

Retention quality weakened. Total ceding (prop + non-prop) increased from 29 Mn to 46 Mn (+17 Mn, +58.6%) while GWP fell, so the ceding ratio rose from 10.6% to 17.4% (+6.8 pp) and retention ratio fell from 89.8% to 82.6% (-7.1 pp).

Mumbai remains the dominant portfolio problem. It still contributes 103 Mn GWP (38.9% of portfolio) and 107 Mn NEP (46.5% of portfolio), but reported -164 Mn UW loss, 235% Financial LR, and 257% COR. Even though its underwriting loss improved from -215 Mn to -164 Mn (+51 Mn), it remains the single largest value destroyer.

Corporate business is the segment-level drag. Corp GWP fell from 186 Mn to 154 Mn (-32 Mn, -17.2%), but UW result worsened from -177 Mn to -192 Mn (-15 Mn) and COR deteriorated from 206% to 237% (+31 pp). This is the portfolio’s largest structural issue.
SME Broking shows classic unprofitable growth. GWP grew from 6 Mn to 14 Mn (+8 Mn, +133.3%), but UW result swung from +1 Mn to -24 Mn (-25 Mn) and COR exploded from 67% to 340% (+273 pp).

Retail remains the best large-scale growth engine, but quality has softened. GWP rose from 66 Mn to 82 Mn (+16 Mn, +24.2%) and UW remained positive at +17 Mn, but COR worsened from 51% to 71% (+20 pp) and Financial LR moved from 7% to 26% (+19 pp).
Emerging is the cleanest segment from a quality standpoint. GWP rose from 15 Mn to 16 Mn (+1 Mn, +6.7%), UW result improved from +3 Mn to +7 Mn (+4 Mn), and COR improved from 62% to 50% (-12 pp).

Delhi is the strongest geography at meaningful scale. GWP grew from 52 Mn to 84 Mn (+32 Mn, +61.5%), NEP from 37 Mn to 67 Mn (+30 Mn, +81.1%), and UW result stayed positive at +12 Mn, although COR worsened from 43% to 82% (+39 pp).

Ahmedabad improved sharply, but the quality of improvement is questionable. UW moved from -21 Mn to +3 Mn (+24 Mn) and COR from 341% to 57% (-284 pp), but this was driven by IBNR moving from +25 Mn to -9 Mn and NIC after IBNR from +25 Mn to -5 Mn, while expense ratio worsened from 19% to 64% (+45 pp).Possible interpretation based on available data: improvement is reserve-release driven rather than purely underwriting-led.

Bangalore and Chennai are high-volatility red flags. Bangalore swung from +46 Mn UW profit to -48 Mn loss (-94 Mn) with COR at 1095%; Chennai moved from +15 Mn to -9 Mn (-24 Mn) with COR at 195%. Both appear highly reserve-sensitive.
Management focus should immediately be on:
validating reserve quality in Bangalore / Chennai / Ahmedabad,
protecting profitable scale in Delhi / Retail / Emerging, and
controlling portfolio acquisition and expense inflation.
GWP declined from 274 Mn to 265 Mn (-9 Mn, -3.3%).
NWP fell from 246 Mn to 219 Mn (-27 Mn, -11.0%), a much steeper decline than GWP.
This happened because total ceding increased from 29 Mn to 46 Mn (+17 Mn, +58.6%), comprising:
Prop ceding:11 Mn to 15 Mn (+4 Mn, +36.4%)
The portfolio is writing slightly less business, but ceding materially more of it. That usually indicates either:
need for more protection on volatile books,
or greater dependence on reinsurance to support capacity.
In this dataset, that is not translating into better underwriting results, so the higher ceding is not yet solving the underlying profitability problem.
NEP stayed almost flat: 231 Mn to 230 Mn (-1 Mn, -0.4%)
PY URR:55 Mn to 70 Mn (+15 Mn, +27.3%)
URR charge:253 Mn to 208 Mn (-45 Mn, -17.8%)
CY release:184 Mn to 150 Mn (-34 Mn, -18.5%)

Although written premium declined and retention weakened, NEP held steady. This means the deterioration in profitability cannot be explained by earned premium collapse. It is much more clearly a function of:
poor portfolio mix in large segments/locations.
NIC before IBNR:147 Mn to 114 Mn (-33 Mn, -22.4%)
IBNR as a share of NIC after IBNR rose from 55.5% to 66.9% (+11.4 pp)
NIC after IBNR:328 Mn to 341 Mn (+13 Mn, +4.0%)
This is the most important portfolio message:
Underlying claims emergence before IBNR improved

This indicates a portfolio that is becoming more dependent on actuarial/reported reserve adequacy rather than clean current-year claims performance. That reduces confidence in the sustainability of improvement.
Acquisition cost:20 Mn to 35 Mn (+15 Mn, +75.0%)
RI commission:3 Mn to 8 Mn (+5 Mn, +166.7%)
Expense in Mn:35 Mn to 54 Mn (+19 Mn, +54.3%)
Sales and operating cost inflation are outpacing the top line:
This is a very clear case of negative operating leverage. Even if claims had been flat, the rise in cost base would still have meaningfully pressured profitability.
UW result:-149 Mn to -191 Mn (-42 Mn, 28.2% larger loss)
UW margin on NEP:-64.5% to -83.0% (-18.5 pp)
flat NEP but worse post-IBNR claims burden
substantially higher expense and acquisition ratios
large structural losses concentrated in Corp / Mumbai
fast growth in some pockets is poor quality (especially SME Broking, Bangalore, Chennai)
The portfolio is not suffering from lack of premium earning, because NEP is stable. It is suffering from:
reserve-heavy claims burden(IBNR +46 Mn, Financial LR +6 pp)
cost inflation(Expense ratio +11 pp, Acq ratio +6 pp)
large-risk / corporate concentration stress(Corp UW loss -192 Mn, Mumbai UW loss -164 Mn)
On a pre-IBNR basis, premium adequacy appears to have improved:
But once IBNR is factored in, adequacy still looks very weak:
This suggests current pricing / selection may not be uniformly poor across every unit, but the portfolio is facing:
or historical underwriting quality issues now surfacing.
IBNR share of total claims:55.5% to 66.9%
A profitable-looking or improving unit needs to be tested carefully for whether the improvement comes from:
a release of earlier reserves / unusually low reserve bookings.
and the prior-year comparatives for Bangalore / Chennai / Delhi
NIC after IBNR:362 Mn to 252 Mn (-110 Mn, -30.4%)
UW result:-215 Mn to -164 Mn (+51 Mn improvement)
Even though absolute UW loss improved by 51 Mn, this is largely because scale collapsed. Margin quality still worsened:

content.text: The Professional Indemnity (PI) portfolio is still profitable, but quality has deteriorated materially. GWP fell from ₹1,084m to ₹702m (-₹382m, -35.2%), while UW result fell from ₹249m to ₹204m (-₹45m, -18.1%), and COR worsened from 51% to 67% (+16 pts).

The headline damage is not from earned premium collapse, but from reserve strengthening and cost dilution. NEP declined only from ₹591m to ₹532m (-₹59m, -10.0%) despite the GWP drop, but IBNR moved from a release of ₹(2)m to a charge of ₹57m (+₹59m adverse), pushing NIC after IBNR from ₹108m to ₹151m (+₹43m, +39.8%).
Retention is broadly stable; ceding is not the core issue at portfolio level. NWP/GWP moved marginally from 66.0% to 65.4% (-0.6 pts), while total ceding moved from 34.0% to 34.5% (+0.4 pts).

Expense efficiency worsened sharply. Expense in absolute terms reduced from ₹95m to ₹82m (-₹13m, -13.7%), but expense ratio rose from 13% to 18% (+5 pts) because the top line shrank much faster than the cost base.

Acquisition quality also weakened. Acquisition cost reduced from ₹174m to ₹131m (-₹43m, -24.7%), but acquisition cost ratio rose from 16% to 19% (+3 pts) and commission ratio rose from 19% to 21% (+2 pts).

Mumbai and Delhi still dominate the portfolio, but their trajectories diverged sharply. Mumbai GWP fell from ₹560m to ₹305m (-₹255m, -45.5%) yet UW result improved from ₹18m to ₹109m (+₹91m) and COR improved from 78% to 45% (-33 pts). Delhi GWP fell from ₹298m to ₹196m (-₹102m, -34.2%), but UW result collapsed from ₹234m to ₹20m (-₹214m) and COR deteriorated from 16% to 98% (+82 pts).

Best total-portfolio quality improvement came from Chennai, Mumbai and Pune. Chennai COR improved from 35% to 19% (-16 pts) with GWP growth of +10%; Mumbai improved 78% to 45% (-33 pts) at very high scale; Pune improved 126% to 55% (-71 pts), though acquisition costs became excessive.

Key underwriting stress sits in Delhi, SME Broking and parts of Emerging. Total PI SME Broking COR worsened from 66% to 103% (+37 pts) and UW result moved from ₹11m profit to ₹(4)m loss. Emerging stayed near break-even at 91% COR, but its ILR jumped from 21% to 64% (+43 pts).

Retail still generates the largest absolute underwriting profit, but quality has clearly weakened. Total PI Retail UW result fell from ₹216m to ₹126m (-₹90m); COR worsened from 26% to 61% (+35 pts); acquisition cost ratio worsened from 24% to 28% (+4 pts) and expense ratio from 13% to 19% (+6 pts).
By sub-product, Healthcare/MPL has weakened meaningfully, FS PI is stable but cost-heavy, and Engineers PI has normalized sharply after an unsustainably favorable prior year.
Healthcare/MPL: COR 61% to 76% (+15 pts); UW result ₹116m to ₹80m (-₹36m).
Financial Services PI: COR 71% to 75% (+4 pts); UW result flat at ₹31m.
Engineers PI: COR -62% to 68% (+130 pts); UW result ₹160m to ₹49m (-₹111m), largely because prior year was flattered by large reserve releases.

There is a strong signal of dependency on reserve movements in several cells. Negative or unusually low loss ratios in prior year (Delhi total PI, Engineers Retail, Engineers Delhi, Hyderabad, Kolkata, etc.) indicate reserve releases or prior-year favorable development. Management should not extrapolate these as sustainable pricing adequacy.

RM strategy should shift from gross-volume recovery to profitable rebuilding. The data supports pushing Chennai, Mumbai (selectively), Corporate PI, Engineers Retail, FS PI Retail, while tightening or repricing Delhi, total SME Broking, FS SME Broking, Healthcare Emerging/Retail pockets, and high-acquisition-cost cells such as Indore and Pune.
Diagnose the Delhi deterioration and reserve swing.
Stop unpriced or over-commissioned growth in SME Broking / high-acquisition-cost cells.
Validate whether Mumbai’s improvement is structurally sustainable or helped by mix/pruning.
Re-underwrite Healthcare Emerging/Retail and Engineers Corporate/Delhi.
Build a dashboard that separates earned profitability from reserve effects.
NWP fell from ₹715m to ₹459m (-₹256m, -35.8%), broadly in line with GWP decline.
The portfolio therefore suffered a large top-line contraction, but not a disproportionate retention collapse.

This is a shrinking portfolio, not a portfolio losing underwriting profitability because of reinsurance leakage alone. The immediate commercial issue is scale loss, especially concentrated in major hubs like Mumbai and Delhi.
Ceding ratio (total ceding / GWP) moved from 34.0% to 34.5% (+0.4 pts).
Retention ratio (NWP/GWP) moved from 66.0% to 65.4% (-0.6 pts).
Reinsurance structure appears broadly stable at portfolio level.
There is no evidence that reinsurance deterioration is the main driver of weaker profitability.
However, several sub-products have very different reinsurance profiles:
FS PI remains highly ceded (58.1% to 53.4% ceding ratio),
Engineers PI is moderately ceded (30.1% to 30.4%).
The profitability conversation should focus more on claims emergence, reserve movement and expense dilution than on retention leakage.

The portfolio is deteriorating in quality, though it remains underwriting-profitable.

Is the portfolio improving or deteriorating?Deteriorating overall. UW result is still positive (₹204m), but margin compressed sharply with COR rising from 51% to 67%, driven by reserve strengthening and cost dilution.
Is growth profitable or poor quality?Overall there is no growth — there is sharp top-line contraction. Where growth does exist, it is mixed:
profitable growth: Chennai total PI, FS Retail, some Engineers Retail/Chennai pockets
poor-quality growth: SME Broking (especially FS SME), some books with rising acquisition ratios or claims deterioration.
Any business requiring very high acquisition cost / commission without adequate loss performance

Bottom line:This is not a broken portfolio, but it is no longer a “coast on prior reserve releases” portfolio. Management should shift from top-line focus to reserve-clean, cost-disciplined, segment-specific underwriting and RM strategy.

content.text: Critical caution on interpretation: FY26 E&O contains negative NWP / negative NEP in Bangalore, Delhi, and Corporate vertical, and very large negative NIC / negative loss ratios in Mumbai and Chennai. In such cases, loss ratios, expense ratios, commission ratios and COR become mathematically distorted because the denominator (NEP) is very small or negative.I will still quote the reported figures, but I will explicitly flag where the ratios are not economically interpretable and where the real story is in the absolute movements.

E&O has deteriorated sharply and is now structurally loss-making. UW result worsened from ₹(322)m to ₹(1,275)m (-₹953m deterioration, +296% deeper loss), while reported COR exploded from 163% to 3,336% (+3,173 pts).

The single biggest portfolio shock is reinsurance / ceding economics, not just claims. NWP collapsed from ₹505m to ₹21m (-₹484m, -95.8%), because non-prop ceding increased from ₹282m to ₹470m (+₹188m, +66.7%) even as GWP fell ₹393m (-37.8%).
Retention economics effectively broke down. Retention (NWP/GWP) fell from 48.6% to 3.2% (-45.3 pts), while total ceding rose from 51.5% to 96.8% (+45.2 pts).
Earned premium also collapsed. NEP fell from ₹511m to ₹44m (-₹467m, -91.4%). That means a large claims and expense base is now being absorbed by a tiny earned-premium denominator.

Claims worsened heavily in absolute terms: NIC before IBNR rose from ₹397m to ₹875m (+₹478m, +120.4%), and IBNR rose from ₹267m to ₹318m (+₹51m, +19.1%), taking NIC after IBNR to ₹1,193m (+₹529m, +79.7%).

The main loss source is Corporate E&O, where GWP fell from ₹898m to ₹476m (-₹422m, -47.0%), but non-prop ceding jumped from ₹239m to ₹442m (+₹203m, +84.9%), NWP turned from ₹412m to ₹(115)m (-₹527m), NEP from ₹426m to ₹(75)m (-₹501m), and UW loss deepened from ₹(305)m to ₹(1,321)m (-₹1,016m).

Delhi is the largest individual red flag. Delhi UW result moved from ₹130m profit to ₹(1,045)m loss (-₹1,175m swing). NEP fell from ₹96m to ₹(33)m (-₹129m) while NIC rose from ₹(62)m to ₹992m (+₹1,054m).

Bangalore is the second-biggest problem center. GWP fell from ₹345m to ₹160m (-₹185m, -53.6%), but non-prop ceding surged from ₹84m to ₹265m (+₹181m, +215.5%), pushing NWP from ₹173m to ₹(168)m (-₹341m) and NEP from ₹212m to ₹(176)m (-₹388m); UW loss worsened from ₹(242)m to ₹(537)m (-₹295m).

Retail is the only meaningful vertical showing genuinely improved business quality. GWP rose from ₹118m to ₹145m (+₹27m, +22.9%), NEP from ₹66m to ₹102m (+₹36m, +54.5%), NIC fell from ₹32m to ₹26m (-₹6m, -18.8%), and UW result improved from ₹(9)m to ₹29m (+₹38m); COR improved from 106% to 66% (-40 pts).

SME Broking also improved on reported numbers, with UW result from ₹(8)m to ₹17m (+₹25m) and COR from 148% to -22% (-170 pts), but this is low-scale and reserve-release dependent because NIC moved from ₹19m to ₹(8)m.
Mumbai and Chennai show very strong reported turnarounds, but both are reserve-release / negative-claims driven in FY26. These are not cleanly sustainable without a claims-triangle view.

Expense efficiency collapsed at portfolio level despite only a small increase in absolute expense. Expense rose from ₹62m to ₹69m (+₹7m, +11.3%), but because NEP collapsed, expense ratio jumped from 12% to 331% (+319 pts).
Acquisition cost reduced in absolute terms from ₹136m to ₹89m (-₹47m, -34.6%), but the acquisition cost ratio still worsened from 13% to 14% (+1 pt) because the premium base collapsed.
Immediate management priority is not growth. It is to diagnose and correct:
whether Mumbai/Chennai improvement is real or reserve-release-led, and
whether E&O should be rebuilt around Retail rather than Corporate-heavy mix.
This is not just a shrinking portfolio. It is a portfolio where net retained economics have almost disappeared.
Total ceding / GWP moved from 51.5% to 96.8% (+45.2 pts)
This is the most important structural change in the book. The E&O portfolio has effectively moved from half-retained to almost fully ceded / overburdened by reinsurance cost.
Portfolio-level earnings capacity is severely damaged.
or accounting allocations that have pulled reinsurance charges disproportionately into E&O.Possible interpretation based on available data.
PY URR was broadly stable: ₹238m to ₹231m (-₹7m, -2.9%)
Even though written premium dropped 37.8%, earned premium dropped 91.4%, because retained net premium and earned net premium were overwhelmed by ceding economics.
The book is no longer generating enough earned premium to absorb claims or operating costs. This makes all ratio deterioration mechanically extreme.
NIC before IBNR rose from ₹397m to ₹875m (+₹478m, +120.4%)
NIC after IBNR rose from ₹664m to ₹1,193m (+₹529m, +79.7%)
NEP collapsed, which magnified ratios beyond normal interpretability.
This is not a denominator-only issue. Even on absolute terms, the claims burden worsened materially.
Expense ratio went from 12% to 331% (+319 pts)
Acquisition cost ratio still worsened from 13% to 14% (+1 pt)
Commission ratio rose from 21% to 274% (+253 pts)
The absolute expense base was not radically higher, but with NEP falling to ₹44m, all cost ratios have blown out.
The book currently has no expense absorption capacity. Even if claims normalize, the earning base is too small unless retention economics are repaired.
The E&O portfolio has deteriorated drastically.This is not a modest profitability issue — it is a fundamental structural breakdown in portfolio economics driven by:
E&O is no longer just “loss-making”; it is economically unstable in FY26 because:
large sections of the portfolio show negative NWP/NEP,
and reported improvements in some locations are driven by negative NIC / reserve release patterns.
These all show negative NIC or very low/negative loss ratios, which strongly suggests reserve releases or recoveries.
The overall portfolio wrote ₹647m GWP, but retained only ₹21m NWP and earned only ₹44m NEP while carrying ₹1,193m NIC.
This is a book where premium adequacy is overwhelmed by net retention economics and claims burden. You cannot correct this with incremental pricing alone. It needs:
SME Broking NIC moved from ₹19m to ₹(8)m (-₹27m)
These cells are benefiting from negative incurred claims, which usually indicates reserve release, favorable development, recoveries, or accounting adjustments.
These should not be interpreted as proof that the underlying current-year underwriting is exceptionally strong.

content.text: The Cyber Liability portfolio has deteriorated sharply on underwriting quality despite only a moderate top-line decline. GWP fell from ₹1,424m to ₹1,228m (-₹196m, -13.8%), but NEP fell much faster from ₹539m to ₹285m (-₹254m, -47.1%), while NIC after IBNR rose from ₹135m to ₹430m (+₹295m, +218.5%), driving UW result from ₹327m profit to ₹210m loss (-₹537m swing) and COR from 47% to 175% (+128 pts).

The core problem is not just lower premium; it is weaker earning conversion plus materially worse claims/reserve experience. Retention reduced from 24.7% to 21.7% (-3.0 pts), ceding increased from 75.3% to 78.2% (+2.9 pts), and NEP/GWP dropped from 37.9% to 23.2% (-14.6 pts).

The reserve position turned sharply adverse. IBNR moved from a ₹14m release to a ₹196m charge (+₹210m adverse swing), converting a relatively benign FY25 claims outcome into a much heavier FY26 incurred burden.
Claims deterioration is very concentrated. The two largest underwriting drags are Bangalore and Mumbai:
Bangalore: UW result ₹19m to ₹(338)m (-₹357m), NIC ₹31m to ₹398m (+₹367m), COR 80% to 559% (+479 pts).
Mumbai: UW result ₹290m to ₹16m (-₹274m), NIC ₹(98)m to ₹57m (+₹155m), COR -34% to 85% (+119 pts).

Corporate is the dominant source of deterioration. Corporate GWP fell from ₹1,197m to ₹993m (-₹204m, -17.0%), NEP from ₹420m to ₹226m (-₹194m, -46.2%), but NIC surged from ₹144m to ₹443m (+₹299m, +207.6%), pushing UW result from ₹233m to ₹(245)m (-₹478m) and COR from 49% to 210% (+161 pts).

The overall book is still highly reinsured and became even more ceded in FY26. Prop ceding stayed very high at ₹989m to ₹950m (-₹39m, -3.9%), but because GWP shrank, the proportional burden remained heavy. Non-prop reduced sharply ₹83m to ₹10m (-₹73m, -88.0%), so the deterioration is not due to non-prop in FY26; it is primarily due to claims and lower earnings conversion.
Best underwriting-quality pockets are Chennai, Delhi, SME Broking and to a smaller extent Kolkata.
Chennai: COR 57% to 3% (-54 pts), ILR 23% to 10% (-13 pts), UWR still positive ₹13m.
Delhi: COR 26% to -21% (-47 pts) with NIC improving from ₹7m to ₹(28)m (-₹35m), though expense ratio worsened from 22% to 38% (+16 pts).
SME Broking: COR 15% to -53% (-68 pts) with UWR still positive at ₹11m.

Most positive reported improvements are not growth-led. Many improving cells still show lower GWP / lower NEP, meaning the profitability improvement came from claims release / reserve release / portfolio shrinkage, not from strong-quality expansion.
There is very limited evidence of healthy growth. The only notable gross growth areas are:
Emerging: GWP ₹2m to ₹7m (+₹5m, +250%), but scale is immaterial and COR worsened to 77%.
SME Broking: GWP ₹28m to ₹31m (+₹3m, +10.7%), UWR stayed positive.
Hyderabad: GWP ₹47m to ₹49m (+₹2m, +4.3%), with a strong UWR recovery from ₹(107)m to ₹27m, but this is clearly reserve-release driven.
Expense efficiency deteriorated materially. Expense rose from ₹122m to ₹130m (+₹8m, +6.6%), but expense ratio rose from 35% to 49% (+14 pts) because NEP nearly halved.

Acquisition burden also worsened despite slightly lower absolute acquisition cost. Acq cost fell from ₹186m to ₹180m (-₹6m, -3.2%), but Acq Cost % rose from 13% to 15% (+2 pts) due to lower premium base.
Management should not read the FY26 deterioration as broad-based market failure. It is a concentrated portfolio-management problem:
Mumbai underwriting normalization after abnormally favorable FY25
Weak NEP conversion because the book is too heavily ceded
Immediate focus should be on underwriting repair, not top-line recovery. The management agenda should be:
diagnose Bangalore and Corporate claims deterioration
keep RMs away from low-quality large-account volume.
The top-line decline is moderate, but the earned premium decline is severe. This means the book is not merely shrinking — it is earning materially less retained premium.
The portfolio’s ability to absorb volatility weakened significantly because the denominator (NEP) nearly halved.
Even though absolute ceding fell, it did not fall fast enough relative to GWP. The book is still heavily reinsured and became slightly more ceded in FY26.
This is a structurally low-retention portfolio. Underwriting profit is therefore highly sensitive to:
claims volatility on a small retained base,
The book is converting written premium into earned premium much less efficiently in FY26.
This is a major red flag: portfolio growth quality weakened even before considering claims, because the earning base reduced sharply.
This is the heart of the issue:
reported claims before IBNR worsened materially,
and reserve position worsened even more sharply.
The FY26 deterioration is not just from weaker premium earning. It is also a real claims/reserving problem, especially because IBNR swung by +₹210m.

Is growth profitable or poor quality?Mostly poor-quality or limited-quality. There is very little broad-based healthy growth. Where growth exists, it is either:

content.text: Also highly recommended as secondary KPIs:
Waterfall chart: FY25 to FY26 UW Result bridge
Prop ceding vs Non-prop vs NWP by segment/location
NIC before IBNR and IBNR by geography/segment
If you want, I can next convert this into either:
a PI vs E&O vs EPL vs Cyber comparative portfolio review, or
a Power BI dashboard blueprint with KPI formulas and page layout.
Portfolio has contracted and profitability has sharply reversed in 4056 / 4056-IIO / 4056-LT Cyber Liability Insurance.GWP fell 14% from 1,424m to 1,228m, while NEP fell 47% from 539m to 285m.
Earnings quality deteriorated materially.UW Result moved from +327m to -210m, Financial LR increased from 25% to 151%, Incurred LR from 28% to 82%, and COR worsened from 47% to 175%.
The issue is not only growth; it is portfolio quality and reserve volatility.IBNR moved from -14m to +196m and NIC after IBNR rose from 135m to 430m, indicating a major adverse reserve swing.
Corporate is the core drag on the book.The Corporate vertical contributes ~81% of GWP (993m of 1,228m) but posted UW loss of 245m with COR at 210%.
Performance is highly concentrated.Mumbai, Bangalore and Delhi contribute ~80% of GWP, which means adverse movement in one or two large zones is materially reshaping portfolio outcome.
Immediate underwriting reset is required in Corporate, especially Bangalore, Mumbai and Ahmedabad.
Cyber portfolio needs stricter technical pricing discipline.No more rate-neutral renewals on accounts with poor loss emergence, reserve stress, or weak structure.

Account-level action should replace broad portfolio action.Every account with high loss ratio / adverse IBNR development / repeated reserve strengthening should go through re-price / restructure / reduce line / exit decisioning.

Reinsurance support is not enough to absorb underlying weakness.While RI Commission improved (231m to 244m) and prop ceding stayed high, this did not offset the sharp deterioration in losses and reserving.

Underwriting quality must be judged on clean current-year economics, not reserve releases.Some better-performing zones show good reported result, but leadership should distinguish underlying margin from reserve-led benefit.
Protect profitable relationships, but do not protect unprofitable economics.
Growth should be selective, not broad-based.Push for growth in Delhi, Chennai, Hyderabad, Retail and SME Broking, where current economics are stronger.
For red-zone accounts, renewal must be conditional.Renewal only if there is adequate rate uplift, tighter terms, lower line size, improved deductible, or reduced coverage scope.
Relationship teams must lead remediation, not only new business.RM scorecards should include:
and portfolio quality, not only topline.
Broker and client conversations must become portfolio-led and fact-based.Large adverse zones need a clear message: capacity will follow profitability, not station presence or relationship history.

children.title: 5. Top 5 concerns : Zone and Vertical Wise

content.text: BangaloreLargest value leak in the portfolio. UW moved from +19m to -338m, Financial LR jumped to 540%, and COR worsened to 559%.This is the single biggest driver of portfolio deterioration.

MumbaiLargest zone by scale (377m GWP) but earnings sharply weakened. GWP down 20%, UW fell from +290m to +16m, and COR worsened from -34% to 85%.Scale is being retained, but margin quality has weakened materially.
AhmedabadSmall book but structurally adverse. Financial LR 342%, Incurred LR 291%, COR 433%, and UW still negative at -22m.Loss experience remains unacceptable despite some YoY improvement.
CorporateBiggest structural concern in the book. GWP 993m, UW -245m, Financial LR 196%, COR 210%.The overall portfolio loss is fundamentally a Corporate vertical problem.

Retail margin compressionRetail remains profitable, but economics are softening. UW fell from +81m to +24m, Expense Ratio rose from 49% to 57%, and COR edged up to 50%.This is an early warning sign on a sizeable book.
Set up a 30-day remediation war room for Bangalore, Mumbai and Ahmedabad.Focus on top loss-making accounts, reserve movements, renewal actions and broker strategy.
Mandate underwriting sign-off for all Corporate renewals above defined premium / exposure thresholds.No renewal without:
Run account-level reserve review with Claims + Actuarial + UW.Priority on Bangalore and Corporate vertical, where reserve movement is distorting portfolio performance.
Stop price-led growth in stressed pockets.Freeze new business expansion / flat renewals / broad relationship concessions in red zones until loss ratio normalises.
Reallocate growth capacity toward profitable engines.Push selectively in Delhi, Chennai, Hyderabad, Retail and SME Broking.
Tighten expense and distribution discipline.Expense Ratio rose to 49% and Acquisition Cost % rose to 15%.Review channel economics, broker payouts, and operating cost intensity by zone.
